name: ElevenLabs
overview: ElevenLabs is an AI voice and audio company founded in 2022 by Piotr Dabkowski (ex-Google) and Mati Staniszewski (ex-Palantir), both Polish, motivated partly by the poor quality of dubbed films they grew up with. Its flagship is realistic text-to-speech and voice cloning capable of conveying emotion and intonation across dozens of languages. The platform expanded into a Dubbing Studio, sound-effects generation, a conversational AI agent layer, and the Reader app for listening to articles and books. By 2025 it had raised large rounds (Series B and C) reaching multi-billion-dollar valuations and became the de facto voice layer for many AI apps. Customers range from indie creators to publishers, game studios, and enterprises.
strategy: ElevenLabs monetizes through usage-based subscription tiers (character/credit quotas) for individuals and creators, plus enterprise and API plans priced on volume for developers embedding voice into their products. Its API-first go-to-market made it the default voice provider for countless agent, chatbot, and content startups, creating a developer flywheel. It pursues both B2C (Reader, voice library, creator tools) and B2B (dubbing for media companies, conversational AI for support). A voice marketplace lets verified actors license their voices and earn payouts, aligning incentives with the talent supply.
differentiation: Its core edge is audio quality and emotional naturalness that has repeatedly led blind-listening comparisons, plus low-latency streaming suitable for real-time conversational agents. Voice cloning from short samples and a large multilingual catalog (70+ languages) let creators preserve a speaker's identity across languages in dubbing. Unlike big-cloud TTS that sounds robotic, ElevenLabs targets expressive, production-grade audio. It has invested heavily in safety tooling (voice verification, AI speech classifier, no-go voice policies) to address deepfake concerns that dog the category.
roadmap: The company is pushing deeper into conversational AI agents, turning voice from a one-shot synthesis tool into a full real-time interaction layer with turn-taking and tool use. Expansion of Dubbing Studio, music/sound-effects generation, and the Reader consumer app point to a broader 'audio platform' ambition. Expect continued language coverage, lower latency for live agents, and enterprise compliance features. It is positioning to be the voice infrastructure underneath the agent wave rather than a single app.
rationale: The founders bet that voice was the most under-served modality in generative AI and that quality, not breadth, would win a category dominated by mediocre incumbent TTS. They chose an API-first model so that being embedded everywhere compounds faster than building a single consumer app, while still seeding consumer products to demonstrate the tech. Heavy safety investment is a deliberate hedge: voice cloning is the most abuse-prone modality, and credibility with media partners requires it. The thesis is that as AI agents become conversational, whoever owns the most natural, lowest-latency voice becomes indispensable infrastructure.
references:
Founders: Mati Staniszewski (CEO), Piotr Dabkowski (CTO)
Flagship: Text-to-Speech, Voice Cloning, Dubbing Studio, Conversational AI, Reader app
https://elevenlabs.io
https://elevenlabs.io/blog
https://elevenlabs.io/docs

name: Suno
overview: Suno is a generative music company founded in 2023 in Cambridge, Massachusetts by Mikey Shulman, Georg Kucsko, Martin Camacho, and Keenan Freyberg, several of whom came from Kensho. Its product generates full songs — vocals, lyrics, instrumentation, and structure — from a text prompt in seconds. Suno reached mainstream virality and partnered with Microsoft to bring music generation into Copilot. By 2024–2025 it had raised a large Series B (reportedly around $125M) and built a broad consumer base of hobbyists, marketers, and creators.
strategy: Suno runs a freemium consumer subscription model: free daily generation credits, with Pro and Premier tiers unlocking more songs, commercial-use rights, and faster generation. It is consumer-first and viral by design, leaning on social sharing of generated tracks to grow. Distribution partnerships (e.g., Microsoft Copilot) extend reach beyond its own app. It targets the long tail of people who want to make music but lack instruments or training, rather than competing head-on with professional DAWs.
differentiation: Suno's edge is end-to-end song generation that produces coherent, catchy, full-length tracks with convincing vocals — a step beyond instrument-only or loop generators. Its model handles song structure (verse/chorus), genre fidelity, and lyric-to-melody alignment unusually well, making outputs feel like real songs rather than samples. The product's speed and low friction make iteration playful. This consumer-grade polish set it apart early from research-grade music models.
roadmap: Suno is improving audio fidelity, vocal realism, and finer creative control (stems, editing, persona/voice consistency) to move from novelty toward a usable creation tool. Expanding commercial licensing and rights clarity is central given litigation pressure from the music industry. Deeper platform integrations and creator-monetization features are likely. The strategic question it must navigate is licensing deals with rights holders versus continued fair-use defense.
rationale: Suno bet that the biggest unlock in music AI was full-song generation for non-musicians, not tools for professionals, because the addressable audience of would-be creators dwarfs the working-musician market. It prioritized a magical consumer experience and virality over enterprise sales, reasoning that delight drives adoption in a creative category. The team accepted significant legal risk inherent to training on music, wagering that the technology and user demand would force the industry toward licensing rather than prohibition. Speed and accessibility were chosen over the controllability that professionals demand.
references:
Founders: Mikey Shulman (CEO), Georg Kucsko, Martin Camacho, Keenan Freyberg
Flagship: Suno song generation app, Microsoft Copilot integration
https://suno.com
https://suno.com/blog

name: Udio
overview: Udio is a generative music app launched in 2024 by Uncharted Labs, a startup founded by former Google DeepMind researchers including Andrew Sanchez and David Ding. Like Suno, it turns text prompts into full songs with vocals and instrumentation, and it gained early attention for high audio fidelity and the viral 'BBL Drizzy' track. It raised an early round backed by investors including Andreessen Horowitz and notable music-world figures such as will.i.am and Common. Its product centers on rapid, prompt-driven song creation with iterative extension and remixing.
strategy: Udio uses a freemium subscription model with monthly generation credits and paid tiers for higher volume, better quality, and commercial rights. It is consumer-creator focused and grows through shareable outputs and community remixing. Positioned directly against Suno as the other leading consumer music-generation app, it competes on output quality and creative control. Like its rival, it depends on conversion of free users to paid subscriptions.
differentiation: Udio differentiated early on perceived audio quality and the ability to extend, remix, and refine tracks section by section, giving creators more iterative control over a song's evolution. Its DeepMind research pedigree informed model architecture choices aimed at fidelity. The product emphasizes editing-style workflows (extend intro/outro, inpaint sections) rather than only one-shot generation. Backing from working musicians lent it credibility in a contested space.
roadmap: Udio is investing in finer-grained editing, stem separation, vocal control, and quality improvements to make outputs more production-ready. Navigating the music industry's litigation and pivoting toward licensing arrangements is a defining strategic priority. Expect continued feature parity competition with Suno and a push toward tools that satisfy more serious creators. Commercial-rights clarity and rights-holder partnerships will shape its viability.
rationale: Udio's founders, coming from frontier audio research at DeepMind, bet they could win the consumer music category on raw output quality and controllability. They chose a creator-iteration model — extend and remix — believing that giving users authorship over the evolving song beats pure one-shot novelty. Like Suno, they accepted heavy legal exposure from training on copyrighted music, wagering that demand and technical inevitability would push the industry toward licensing. The approach assumes quality and editing depth are the durable moats once the novelty fades.
references:
Company: Uncharted Labs
Founders include Andrew Sanchez, David Ding (ex-Google DeepMind)
Flagship: Udio music-generation app
https://www.udio.com
https://www.udio.com/blog

name: Synthesia
overview: Synthesia is an AI video generation platform founded in 2017 by Victor Riparbelli, Steffen Tjerrild, and academics Matthias Niessner and Lourdes Agapito. It lets users create studio-quality videos from text using realistic AI avatars and voiceovers in 120+ languages, with no cameras, actors, or studios required. It is primarily used for corporate training, internal communications, sales enablement, and product/marketing videos. By 2025 it had raised large rounds (Series C/D) reaching a multi-billion-dollar valuation and counted a large share of the Fortune 100 among customers. It is one of London's most prominent AI companies.
strategy: Synthesia sells primarily to enterprises via tiered SaaS subscriptions (Starter/Creator/Enterprise) plus custom plans, monetizing on video minutes, avatars, seats, and brand/collaboration features. Its go-to-market is B2B-led with a strong self-serve funnel for teams, focused on replacing expensive video production and translating L&D content at scale. It offers personal/custom avatars and a library of stock avatars, plus enterprise governance, SSO, and brand controls. The wedge is corporate learning and communications where translated, updatable video has clear ROI.
differentiation: Synthesia's edge is enterprise-grade, governable AI video built around avatars and a polished editor rather than open-ended cinematic generation. It invested early and heavily in trust and safety — consent-based avatar creation, content moderation, and refusing political/abusive use — which made it palatable to large, risk-averse companies. Multilingual translation of a single video into 100+ languages is a concrete enterprise value driver. Its focus on talking-head business video, not Hollywood-style scenes, gives it reliability and consistency competitors chasing realism struggle to match.
roadmap: Synthesia is advancing avatar expressiveness, full-body and gesture-rich avatars, and more interactive video (clickable, agent-like experiences). It is moving toward an AI 'video agent' and broader content workflows, integrating with LMS and enterprise tools. Continued investment in safety/compliance supports enterprise expansion and regulatory readiness. Expect deeper personalization, real-time avatar interactions, and expansion beyond pre-rendered video toward conversational avatar experiences.
rationale: Synthesia bet that the durable market for AI video was enterprise communication, not viral consumer clips, because businesses spend enormously on training and localization that AI can compress dramatically. It deliberately constrained its product to avatars and corporate use cases — and built strong consent/safety guardrails — reasoning that trust, not spectacle, is what unlocks Fortune 500 budgets. This choice trades the hype of cinematic generation for predictable, defensible enterprise revenue. The thesis: in a deepfake-anxious world, the company that makes AI video safe and boring-reliable wins the largest, most willing-to-pay customers.
references:
Founders: Victor Riparbelli (CEO), Steffen Tjerrild, Matthias Niessner, Lourdes Agapito
Flagship: Synthesia AI video platform, AI avatars
https://www.synthesia.io
https://www.synthesia.io/blog

name: HeyGen
overview: HeyGen is an AI video generation company founded in 2020 (originally as Movio) by Joshua Xu and Wayne Liang, with roots connecting to teams in China and the US. It creates videos with realistic AI avatars and voices and is best known for its viral video translation feature that lip-syncs a speaker into other languages while preserving their voice. Its products include custom avatars, an avatar/clip generator, interactive avatars, and an API for developers. By 2024–2025 it had raised a Series A from Benchmark and reached a roughly $500M+ valuation amid rapid ARR growth. It is popular with marketers, creators, and businesses producing localized video at scale.
strategy: HeyGen monetizes via subscription tiers based on video credits, avatar count, and resolution, plus an API and enterprise plans for developers and businesses. Its go-to-market blends product-led, creator-driven virality (its translation demos spread widely) with growing enterprise sales. It targets marketing, sales outreach, localization, and personalized video at scale, including programmatic/personalized video via API. Faster iteration and aggressive feature shipping define its competitive posture against Synthesia.
differentiation: HeyGen's standout is high-quality video translation with accurate lip-sync that makes a speaker appear to natively speak another language, which became a breakout viral moment for the category. It moves fast on consumer-friendly avatar features, including expressive and interactive (real-time) avatars and quick custom-avatar creation from short footage. It leans more creator- and marketing-oriented and developer-accessible (API) compared to Synthesia's enterprise-governance focus. Speed and a steady stream of viral-ready features are its growth engine.
roadmap: HeyGen is pushing toward interactive, real-time avatars and avatar 'agents' that can converse, plus higher-fidelity and full-body avatars. Deeper API and platform plays target programmatic personalized video and embedding avatars into other products. Expanding enterprise features and safeguards is needed as it scales upmarket. Expect continued investment in translation quality, real-time generation, and creator monetization.
rationale: HeyGen bet that virality and developer accessibility would win share faster than enterprise-first sales, using breakout features like translation to seed mass awareness. It chose a broad creator-plus-API strategy on the thesis that AI video becomes a utility embedded everywhere, not just a closed enterprise tool. Prioritizing speed and shareable wow-moments reflects a belief that mindshare compounds in a crowded category. The wager is that being the fastest, most accessible avatar/translation engine captures the creator and SMB long tail that grows into enterprise.
references:
Founders: Joshua Xu (CEO), Wayne Liang
Flagship: AI avatars, Video Translation, Interactive Avatars, HeyGen API
https://www.heygen.com
https://www.heygen.com/blog

name: Character.AI
overview: Character.AI is a consumer platform for chatting with user-created AI 'characters' — personas ranging from fictional figures to helpers and companions — founded in 2021 by Noam Shazeer and Daniel De Freitas, who previously worked on Google's LaMDA. It became one of the most-used consumer AI apps by engagement, with users spending unusually long session times conversing with characters. In August 2024, Google struck a deal reportedly worth around $2.7B to license its technology and bring Shazeer and De Freitas (and key staff) back to Google DeepMind. The company continued operating its app under new leadership after the deal.
strategy: Character.AI monetizes mainly through 'c.ai+' subscriptions offering faster responses, priority access, and perks, atop a large free tier. Its model is consumer engagement-first: a vast library of user-generated characters drives a content flywheel and very high time-spent. After the Google licensing deal, it shifted toward using third-party/open models rather than solely its own to reduce the cost of training frontier models. Its positioning is entertainment and companionship rather than productivity.
differentiation: Its edge is extraordinarily sticky, emotionally engaging conversational experiences and a community-driven catalog of millions of characters, producing some of the highest session times in consumer apps. The product optimized for personality, roleplay, and ongoing relationships rather than factual assistance, a distinct niche from assistant chatbots. Founders Shazeer (a Transformer co-author) and De Freitas brought deep dialogue-model expertise. The UGC character ecosystem is a moat competitors struggle to replicate.
roadmap: Post-deal, Character.AI is focused on its consumer entertainment app, leaning on external models while emphasizing safety, especially for younger users after lawsuits and scrutiny over harmful interactions. Expect expanded safety controls, age-appropriate experiences, parental features, and possibly new modalities (voice, visuals) for richer characters. The strategic challenge is sustaining engagement and monetization without the founders and without funding frontier model training. Trust and safety is now a defining, existential priority.
rationale: The founders bet that companionship and entertainment, not assistance, would be the killer consumer use of LLMs, because emotional engagement drives retention far beyond Q&A. They prioritized open-ended personality and roleplay over factual accuracy, accepting that a different product than the assistant pack would attract a passionate base. The Google deal reflected a pragmatic recognition that competing on frontier model training was unsustainably expensive; licensing the tech and returning talent to Google captured value while letting the app continue. The remaining thesis is that a UGC character ecosystem can endure on cheaper external models.
references:
Founders: Noam Shazeer, Daniel De Freitas (returned to Google DeepMind, Aug 2024)
Flagship: Character.AI app, user-created characters, c.ai+
https://character.ai
https://blog.character.ai

name: Captions
overview: Captions is an AI video creation company founded in 2021 by Gaurav Misra (ex-Snap design lead) and Dwight Churchill, based in New York. It began as a mobile app for automatically generating accurate, stylized captions and AI-powered editing for talking-head and short-form videos, then expanded into AI avatars, dubbing, and an AI 'creator' product (Mirage) for generating fully AI-generated video presenters. It raised significant funding (including from Kleiner Perkins, Sequoia, Andreessen Horowitz, and Index) reaching a valuation around $500M. Its users are creators, marketers, and businesses making social and ad video.
strategy: Captions monetizes via consumer/creator subscriptions and increasingly business plans, charging for AI features, exports, and avatar/generation usage. It is mobile- and product-led, growing through creators making short-form content and ads. The 2024 push toward 'Mirage' — its own foundation model for AI actors/video — signals a move up the value chain from editing utility to generative video studio. It targets the high-volume world of social video and performance marketing where speed and volume matter.
differentiation: Captions differentiates by owning the full talking-video workflow on mobile — recording, AI editing, eye contact correction, captions, dubbing, and AI avatars — in one tight, creator-friendly app. Its investment in a proprietary generative video model (Mirage) aims to produce expressive AI presenters rather than relying on third-party models. The focus on short-form, face-to-camera content (the dominant social format) gives it a sharply defined wedge. Polished, opinionated UX inherited from Snap design DNA sets it apart from generic editors.
roadmap: Captions is betting on generative AI video at scale via Mirage, aiming to let anyone generate convincing AI actors and ads from prompts. Expect deeper ad-creation tooling for marketers, more avatar realism and control, and expansion from editing into end-to-end generation. Business and API-style use cases for high-volume video ads are a growth vector. The trajectory is from caption utility to a generative video creation platform.
rationale: Captions bet that short-form talking video is the dominant content format and that owning the creator's entire mobile workflow — then layering generative AI on top — beats being a single feature. By building its own video model (Mirage), it wagered that vertical control of the generation stack yields better, more differentiated output than wrapping someone else's model. It chose the high-frequency social-and-ads market because volume and repeat use drive durable consumer/SMB revenue. The thesis: as AI video commoditizes, the winner owns the workflow and the model, not just the editor.
references:
Founders: Gaurav Misra (CEO), Dwight Churchill
Flagship: Captions app, Mirage (generative video model), AI Avatars, AI Ads
https://www.captions.ai
https://www.captions.ai/blog

name: Descript
overview: Descript is an AI-powered audio and video editor founded in 2017 by Andrew Mason (co-founder and former CEO of Groupon), spun out of his podcast company Detour. Its defining idea is editing media by editing a text transcript — delete a word from the doc and it's removed from the audio/video. It pioneered Overdub (an AI voice clone for fixing recordings by typing) and includes screen recording, multitrack editing, filler-word removal, and AI features for podcasts and video. Backed by investors including Andreessen Horowitz and OpenAI's Startup Fund, it serves podcasters, YouTubers, marketers, and teams.
strategy: Descript sells tiered SaaS subscriptions (free, creator, pro, enterprise) gated on transcription hours, AI features, and export quality. It is product-led and creator-focused, with strong adoption among podcasters and video teams who value the text-based editing workflow. It monetizes the time savings of AI editing — removing the steep learning curve of traditional editors like Premiere or Audition. Team/collaboration features extend it into business and agency use.
differentiation: Descript's signature edge is text-based editing of audio and video, which collapses a notoriously hard skill into word-processor simplicity — its single most distinctive capability. Overdub voice cloning and AI tools (eye contact, studio sound, filler removal, automatic clips) compound the time savings. It integrates transcription, recording, editing, and publishing in one app rather than a toolchain. The workflow is uniquely approachable for non-editors, which is its durable wedge.
roadmap: Descript is layering more generative and 'agentic' AI into editing — automatic show creation, AI-assisted clip generation, and underlying its own/partner models to make editing increasingly prompt-driven. Expect deeper video capabilities, AI that drafts edits or whole episodes, and continued investment in collaboration. The direction is from a smart editor toward an AI editing co-pilot that does more of the work automatically. Broadening from podcasts into general video creation is a key growth path.
rationale: Mason bet that the bottleneck in audio/video creation was the editing interface itself, and that representing media as editable text would democratize a skill locked behind complex timelines. Building AI features (Overdub, studio sound) on top of that text foundation reflected a belief that the transcript is the right abstraction for AI to operate on. Descript chose the creator/podcast market because those users feel editing pain acutely and adopt time-saving tools eagerly. The thesis: whoever makes editing as easy as writing captures the exploding population of audio/video creators.
references:
Founder: Andrew Mason (CEO)
Flagship: Descript editor, text-based editing, Overdub, Studio Sound
https://www.descript.com
https://www.descript.com/blog

name: Adobe Firefly
overview: Adobe Firefly is Adobe's family of generative AI models and the brand for its generative features, first introduced in March 2023. It spans image generation, generative fill/expand, vector and design generation, text effects, and increasingly video and audio, surfaced both in a standalone Firefly web app and embedded across Photoshop, Illustrator, Express, and Premiere Pro. Adobe's key positioning is that Firefly's image models were trained on licensed Adobe Stock, openly licensed, and public-domain content, making outputs 'commercially safe.' Firefly generations are paired with Content Credentials provenance metadata.
strategy: Adobe monetizes Firefly through a 'generative credits' system tied to Creative Cloud plans and standalone Firefly subscriptions, plus enterprise offerings with IP indemnification. The strategy is to embed generative AI deep inside Adobe's installed base of creative professionals so generation augments existing workflows rather than replacing the tools. For enterprises, Adobe offers custom-trained models and indemnifies customers against IP claims for Firefly-generated content — a commercial-safety guarantee competitors largely can't match. Firefly is also opening to partner models (e.g., third-party image/video models) inside its app while keeping its own commercially-safe core.
differentiation: Firefly's genuine edge is commercial safety and provenance: training on licensed/Adobe Stock/public-domain data, enterprise IP indemnification, and Content Credentials provenance baked in, which matters enormously to brands and agencies wary of copyright risk. Its second edge is deep, native integration into the tools designers already live in (Photoshop generative fill being the standout), making AI a brush rather than a separate app. Adobe also compensates Stock contributors whose work trains the models, building a defensible, ethically-positioned data supply. This 'safe + embedded' combination is the antithesis of scrape-everything rivals.
roadmap: Adobe is expanding Firefly into video, audio, and a broader creative model lineup, and turning the Firefly app into a hub that also hosts select partner models alongside Adobe's own. Expect more agentic and assistive features inside Creative Cloud, deeper enterprise customization, and continued emphasis on provenance and content authenticity standards. Firefly Video and richer design/vector generation are central bets. The direction is an AI layer spanning Adobe's entire creative and marketing stack (including GenStudio for enterprises).
rationale: Adobe bet that for professional and enterprise creatives, legal safety and trust matter more than raw model novelty, so it deliberately constrained training data to licensed/owned sources and added indemnification and provenance. It chose embedding over a standalone-app-only approach because its moat is the millions of professionals already in Photoshop and Illustrator — generation as a feature defends that base against AI-native disruptors. Compensating contributors and championing Content Credentials reflects a thesis that the durable winner in creative AI is the one brands can deploy without copyright fear. Adobe traded the cutting edge of unrestricted models for the larger prize of safe, integrated, monetizable AI across its ecosystem.
references:
Parent: Adobe; CEO Shantanu Narayen
Flagship: Firefly app, Generative Fill (Photoshop), Firefly Image/Video models, Content Credentials
https://www.adobe.com/products/firefly.html
https://firefly.adobe.com
https://helpx.adobe.com/firefly.html

name: Canva
overview: Canva is the Australian design platform founded in 2013 by Melanie Perkins, Cliff Obrecht, and Cameron Adams, and its generative-AI strategy is delivered through 'Magic Studio,' a suite of AI features layered into its drag-and-drop editor. Magic Studio includes Magic Design (auto-generate designs from a prompt or content), Magic Write (AI copywriting), Magic Media (text-to-image and text-to-video), Magic Edit/Grab/Expand, and translation and brand tools. Canva acquired Leonardo.ai in 2024 to bolster its own generative image models and AI talent, and integrates partner models as well. With hundreds of millions of users, Canva embeds AI for the mass market of non-designers.
strategy: Canva monetizes AI by gating Magic Studio's most powerful features behind Canva Pro and Teams/Enterprise subscriptions, using AI as a key driver to convert free users and expand into businesses. Its go-to-market is mass-market and product-led — AI lowers the skill barrier so anyone can produce professional-looking content, reinforcing Canva's core value proposition. For enterprises, it bundles AI with brand controls, collaboration, and its Work Suite to compete with Microsoft and Adobe. The acquisition of Leonardo.ai gives it owned model capability rather than pure reliance on third parties.
differentiation: Canva's edge is distribution and accessibility: it puts generative AI in front of a massive, non-designer audience inside an editor they already use, so AI becomes a one-click shortcut rather than a separate tool to learn. It combines multiple AI capabilities (design, copy, image, video, translate, edit) in a single approachable workflow tied to templates and brand kits. Owning Leonardo.ai plus integrating partner models lets it offer breadth without betting on one model. Its scale and freemium funnel turn AI into a conversion and retention engine that pure-play generators lack.
roadmap: Canva is deepening AI across its Visual Suite — more agentic 'describe it and Canva builds it' design generation, richer video, and enterprise AI with governance. Expect continued buildout of owned models (via Leonardo.ai talent), tighter brand/enterprise controls, and AI woven into docs, websites, and presentations. It is positioning Magic Studio as the AI layer for everyday visual and document work for teams and businesses. The bet is to become the default AI-powered creation suite for the non-specialist.
rationale: Canva bet that the largest opportunity in creative AI is empowering the billions of people who are not professional designers, so it embeds AI to remove skill barriers rather than chasing professional power-users. It chose to integrate AI into its existing high-engagement editor — leveraging distribution as the moat — because reach and ease-of-use convert better than standalone model quality alone. Acquiring Leonardo.ai while also using partner models reflects a pragmatic 'own enough, integrate the rest' stance to avoid dependence on any single provider. The thesis: in mass-market creation, the winner is whoever makes AI effortless inside the tool people already open every day.
references:
Founders: Melanie Perkins (CEO), Cliff Obrecht, Cameron Adams
Flagship: Canva editor, Magic Studio (Magic Design, Magic Write, Magic Media), Leonardo.ai (acquired 2024)
https://www.canva.com/magic
https://www.canva.com/newsroom
